No cross-zone imports exist between any of the five domain/foundation packages (rex, sourcevision, hench, llm-client) — the four-tier hierarchy is fully respected at the package level, and all coupling is confined to the web package's internal zones

rex and sourcevision each appear as the source of exactly one incoming edge at web-server (via their respective gateway modules), while hench contributes zero incoming edges. This three-way asymmetry reflects the tier contract: rex and sourcevision are consumed via explicit gateways, while hench is accessed exclusively via spawn from orchestration — meaning hench has no import surface in the cross-zone graph at all.

The 'web' residual zone (cohesion 0.32) has bidirectional coupling to web-viewer (7+4 edges) and a unidirectional edge to web-server (1 edge), making it an unintended structural bridge connecting two documented zones. Of all zone pairs in the graph, the web↔web-viewer bidirectional coupling is the only one where a residual catch-all zone participates — eliminating this zone by reclassifying its files into web-viewer or web-server would remove one of the three documented bidirectional couplings.

web-viewer participates in 6 of the 10 distinct directed cross-zone edges (as source or target), confirming hub topology. The practical consequence is that web-viewer's public API changes require coordinated updates across web-server, crash, and web zones simultaneously — the highest coordination cost of any single zone in the monorepo.

All micro-zones (completion-reader-analyzer, prd-epic-resolver-cli, go-project-test-fixtures, web-landing, web-unit) have zero cross-zone edges in both directions. This uniform zero-edge pattern masks three distinct root causes requiring different remediation: orphaned production code (reconnect import or delete), fixture isolation (correct, no action), and test utility zones (expected if directly consuming adjacent production zones via spawn).

No cross-zone edges exist between any two test zones — all test-to-production coupling is strictly unidirectional (test imports production, never the reverse). This confirms test zones do not introduce hidden coupling paths between production zones, which would create transitive dependencies invisible to gateway enforcement.

The web-unit test zone imports from web-server (3 edges) but has no edges to web-viewer, suggesting unit tests directly exercise server internals. If these are intended to test the server's external API surface rather than internals, the direct import edges indicate white-box coupling that could make tests brittle to internal server refactoring.

All five test zones (e2e, integration, web-unit, rex-e2e, web-integration) have zero cross-zone coupling to each other, forming a structurally isolated test island. This is correct and should be preserved as the test suite grows.

The gateway pattern produces an intentionally asymmetric import graph: domain packages (rex, sourcevision) each contribute exactly 1 edge into web-server while web-viewer contributes 88. The asymmetry reflects the gateway constraint working as designed — narrow, auditable domain surfaces vs broad UI aggregation.

web-landing and web-integration (2 files each, cohesion 1) are the smallest named zones in the monorepo. Their minimal size and perfect cohesion confirm they are correctly scoped satellite zones that delegate shared behavior to parent zones rather than accumulating logic — a pattern to preserve as they grow.

The call graph asymmetry (158 calls: web-viewer→web-server; 20 calls: web-build-viewer-extensions→web-server) reveals that runtime activation frequency is not uniform across edges with similar import counts. For the high-frequency web-viewer→web-server interface, a formal API type boundary (a declared interface rather than ad-hoc function imports) would reduce refactoring risk proportional to call volume.

Three of the five inbound edges to web-server (from rex, sourcevision, and web) originate outside the web package. The two domain-package edges (rex, sourcevision) are expected and gateway-mediated; the web residual zone edge is architecturally ambiguous and warrants placement review.

Production source inspection invalidates the highest-severity architectural concern in the prior analysis: server→viewer reverse imports (flagged as 5 edges) do not exist in any server/*.ts file. The web package's server/viewer boundary is strictly unidirectional in production code, resolving the apparent contradiction with CLAUDE.md's static-asset serving model.

The external.ts gateway achieves complete enforcement: 22 viewer files consume cross-zone types through a single file with zero detected bypasses. This is the strongest example in the monorepo of a gateway pattern holding at scale — it should be referenced as the reference implementation for future gateway additions in hench or rex CLI zones.

The 88 'web-viewer→web-server' import edges in zone analysis are unaccounted for by production viewer source code — external.ts imports from schema/ (in web-dashboard-ui-hub, not web-server), and no viewer file imports from server/ directly. These 88 edges require investigation to identify their actual source: likely public.ts, cli/index.ts, or test files classified in web-dashboard-ui-hub that import from web-server. Until resolved, zone-level coupling metrics between these two zones should be treated as unreliable.

The zone-neutral designation of shared-types.ts represents an undocumented architectural pattern — a server-directory type file explicitly designed to serve multiple zones without gateway mediation. If this pattern proliferates without documentation, it creates invisible cross-zone type dependencies that bypass boundary-check tooling. Adding it to CLAUDE.md's gateway registry (or a separate 'neutral types' registry) would make it auditable.

Build scripts (build.js, dev.js) classified in web-build-viewer-extensions have zero source imports — they are structurally disconnected from every other file in their zone. The dual-fragility metrics for this zone are driven entirely by the viewer feature modules, not the build scripts. Any governance action targeting the zone (two-consumer rule, cohesion monitoring) applies only to the viewer-feature sub-group, not to the build tooling.

The rex→web-server and sourcevision→web-server single-import edges in the cross-zone graph have no corresponding source code — no import of @n-dx/web or web-server paths exists in rex or sourcevision src trees; these edges are likely zone-detection artifacts (test files attributed to the wrong production zone) and should be investigated in the zone assignment algorithm before being treated as real tier violations

The crash↔web-viewer circular dependency is the only zone-level cycle in the entire visible cross-zone import graph; all other zone relationships are either unidirectional or mediated through gateways — the uniqueness of this cycle makes it both the highest-priority structural fix and a validation that the gateway pattern is otherwise working correctly across 10+ cross-zone edges

The three test enforcement zones (monorepo-e2e-tests, monorepo-integration-tests, web-package-integration-tests) maintain perfect cohesion and zero production coupling — but they enforce architectural constraints at three distinct scopes that map to the four-tier hierarchy: intra-zone boundaries (web-package-integration), cross-package contracts (monorepo-integration), and tier boundary rules (monorepo-e2e); this layered enforcement is a structural asset that should be explicitly preserved in TESTING.md

The web-viewer zone acts as both the primary import source (88 edges to web-server) and a significant import target (7 from web, 5 from web-server, 2 from crash), making it the highest-traffic hub in the entire cross-zone graph; despite this, it maintains cohesion of 0.97 — confirming that gateway concentration in external.ts is absorbing coupling effectively, but also that web-viewer is a concentration risk if external.ts's import discipline erodes

The monorepo-maintenance-scripts zone is the only zone with zero coupling to any test zone and no production consumers — it exists entirely outside the automated verification envelope; any changes to scripts/ cannot break CI but also cannot be verified as correct by CI

All cross-zone coupling in the monorepo is confined to the web package's internal zones — the four domain/foundation packages (rex, sourcevision, hench, llm-client) have zero cross-package import edges, validating the four-tier hierarchy as structurally sound

web-viewer is the convergence point for all three bidirectional cycles in the codebase — targeted remediation of the web-server→web-viewer reverse direction (5 edges) would reduce the cycle count by one-third without requiring changes to crash or the residual web zone

web-viewer participates in 6 of the 10 distinct cross-zone directed edges, confirming hub topology with the widest blast radius in the monorepo. Any web-viewer public API change requires coordinated updates in web-server, crash, and web zones simultaneously.

The 'web' residual zone (cohesion 0.32) is the only catch-all zone participating in bidirectional coupling (7+4 edges with web-viewer, 1 outgoing to web-server). Reclassifying its files into web-viewer or web-server would eliminate one of the three documented bidirectional coupling pairs and improve overall graph clarity.

All five micro-zones (2 files or fewer) have zero cross-zone edges, but for structurally different reasons: fixture isolation (intentional), orphaned production code (actionable), and detached CLI commands (requires verification). Tooling that flags micro-zones uniformly risks generating noise — severity should be conditioned on whether the zone contains production source files.

Call graph frequency data (158 calls / 88 imports on the web-viewer→web-server edge) shows the primary cross-zone interface is heavily exercised at runtime. Formalizing this interface with an explicit TypeScript contract type would reduce refactoring blast radius proportional to call frequency.

All five fixture sub-zones (go-middleware, go-user-service, go-response-package, mixed-project-go-router, mixed-project-ts-frontend) have zero cross-zone coupling, meaning sourcevision's fixture corpus currently tests only isolated file-group detection. No fixture zone exercises cross-package import graph traversal, leaving a gap in sourcevision's integration test coverage for its most complex analysis capability.

External.ts gateway enforcement is complete: 22 consumers, zero bypasses, single file auditable surface. The pattern should be adopted as the reference implementation for any future intra-package gateway in hench or rex — particularly for the rex-cli zone (27+ command files) where a similar gateway to rex-prd-engine would make cross-zone type dependencies explicit.

The zone-neutral type file pattern (shared-types.ts) is undocumented in CLAUDE.md's gateway registry and invisible to boundary-check tooling. If this pattern is used in more than one location without a registry entry, it will erode the auditability guarantees that the gateway pattern is designed to provide. A 'zone-neutral types' section in CLAUDE.md's gateway module table would close this gap.

The crash↔web-viewer circular dependency is the sole zone-level cycle in the cross-zone import graph; all other inter-zone relationships are strictly unidirectional or gateway-mediated — the cycle's isolation makes it tractable to fix (one type-only import in use-crash-recovery.ts) and its resolution would achieve full DAG topology across the production graph

The three-tier test enforcement pyramid (web-package-integration → monorepo-integration → monorepo-e2e) provides non-overlapping coverage of the four-tier dependency hierarchy without any architectural gaps — this is a mature and intentional test governance structure worth documenting explicitly in TESTING.md

web-viewer's position as the highest-traffic cross-zone hub (net 100+ import edges) combined with high cohesion (0.97) confirms external.ts is successfully acting as an absorption layer — however, the hub position means any gateway bypass in a single leaf file creates an outsized coupling regression, making gateway enforcement tests critical for web-viewer specifically

The residual web zone (cohesion 0.32) participates in bidirectional coupling with both web-viewer (7+4) and web-server (1) while also being the zone that mixes build scripts with viewer feature modules — its structural ambiguity is both a placement artifact and an active coupling risk

Domain packages rex and sourcevision each contribute exactly 1 incoming edge to web-server while hench contributes 0, creating a three-way asymmetry that correctly reflects the tier contract: gateway consumption for domain packages, spawn-only for the execution tier.

crash↔web-viewer forms an undocumented dependency cycle (2 edges each direction). CLAUDE.md sanctions crash→web-shared but not crash→web-viewer. This cycle contradicts the documented unidirectional topology and must be resolved by removing crash→web-viewer imports and routing through web-shared instead.

web-server→web-viewer (5 runtime edges) inverts the documented composition model. web-server is the composition root that wires gateways and serves web-viewer as static assets — runtime imports in the reverse direction make the composition root dependent on its own output, violating the build-time/runtime separation described in CLAUDE.md.

The 88 web-viewer→web-server import edges in zone analysis cannot be reconciled with production source: external.ts (the sole viewer gateway) imports from schema and shared (both in web-dashboard-ui-hub, not web-server), and no viewer file imports directly from server/. The zone analysis edge count may reflect test imports, public entry points, or a zone boundary definition that differs from the directory-based intuition. This discrepancy makes the 88-edge coupling metric unreliable for architectural governance until its source files are identified.

rex→web-server and sourcevision→web-server appear as 1-import edges in the zone graph but have no corresponding source imports in the production code — these are likely zone attribution artifacts where test or fixture files are assigned to the wrong zone; the community detection algorithm should be checked for test file contamination of domain package zones

The shared/ barrel violation exists in two files simultaneously (external.ts importing shared/view-id.js directly, crash-detector.ts importing shared/view-id.js directly) while shared/index.ts already exports ViewId — if boundary-check.test.ts is enforcing the barrel rule, it has a specific gap for viewer/external.ts and viewer/crash/crash-detector.ts consumers; if it is not enforcing for these files, the documented enforcement guarantee is incomplete

api.ts (inbound web-viewer gateway) and external.ts (outbound web-viewer gateway) represent an intentional dual-gateway design but only external.ts has verified enforcement (22 consumers, zero bypasses) — api.ts has zero verified consumers, making it a documentation-only contract rather than an enforced architectural boundary; the asymmetry means outbound coupling is fully auditable but inbound coupling is invisible to static enforcement

The crash zone's physical location inside viewer/crash/ (a sub-directory of the main viewer directory) means the community detection algorithm is treating intra-directory imports as cross-zone coupling — the 2+2 bidirectional crash↔web-viewer edges that have been flagged as a critical cycle throughout prior analysis are likely directory-traversal artifacts rather than genuine cross-boundary imports; this calls into question whether the 'sole zone-level cycle' conclusion is accurate

external.ts bypasses shared/index.ts by importing from ../shared/view-id.js, ../shared/data-files.js, and ../shared/db-packages.js directly — it mirrors the shared/index.ts barrel exactly but as direct leaf imports rather than re-exports from the barrel, creating a duplicate export surface that means future additions to shared/ must be made in both shared/index.ts AND external.ts to stay consistent

analyze/reason.js acts as a monolithic module that serves as both LLM infrastructure provider and PRD analysis domain engine — its 40+ export surface makes it the highest single-module coupling point in the analyze/ pipeline, and its placement in the analyze/ sub-directory rather than a separate llm-infrastructure sub-directory makes it easy for future contributors to add more domain-type exports to an already-overloaded module

The web residual zone (cohesion 0.32) with bidirectional edges to web-viewer (7+4) and an outgoing edge to web-server (1) creates an implicit transitive coupling path: web-server → web → web-viewer. This three-hop path bypasses the server/viewer boundary because it is composed of two independent zone edges rather than a direct import, making it invisible to boundary-check tooling. web-server thus has an indirect runtime dependency on web-viewer that no enforcement mechanism detects.

Hench contributes zero cross-zone edges at the monorepo level, creating a structural blind spot: any dependency audit that relies solely on zone-level cross-package edges will classify hench as dependency-free, missing its actual runtime coupling to rex (via rex-gateway.ts) and llm-client (via llm-gateway.ts). These execution-tier dependencies only surface by inspecting hench's intra-package call graph — a discoverability gap that grows as hench-agent adds new tool categories.

No fixture zone exercises cross-package import graph traversal — all five fixture sub-zones have zero cross-zone coupling, meaning sourcevision's integration tests validate file discovery and isolated zone detection but not multi-hop import path tracing. This leaves a concrete gap in integration coverage for sourcevision's most structurally complex capability.

The crash zone has a dual import footprint: crash→web-shared (documented CLAUDE.md bypass, tested by boundary-check.test.ts) and crash→web-viewer (2 edges, undocumented, contradicts unidirectional topology). boundary-check tests enforce the web-shared exemption but contain no assertion preventing crash→web-viewer from growing — creating an asymmetric enforcement gap where one direction of crash's imports is gated by CI and the other is not.

The crash↔web-viewer cycle identified in zone analysis is likely a phantom caused by zone boundary attribution: crash/ is a subdirectory inside src/viewer/, so the zone detector may be misclassifying viewer-side crash consumers (crash-recovery-banner.ts in src/viewer/components/) as crash zone members. If confirmed via zone file listing, the crash zone has no real cycle and the actual topology is strictly unidirectional (web-viewer → crash), matching CLAUDE.md's documented model. Zone boundary definitions that rely on directory prefix matching rather than import graph partitioning will consistently misattribute files in nested directories.

The CLAUDE.md-documented hench sub-zone topology (agent/, prd/, brief/, tools/, process/) diverges from the actual source tree: brief/ does not exist, prd/ has no barrel index.ts, and additional sub-zones (guard/, queue/, store/, types/, validation/, schema/) exist but are unmentioned. The governance documentation describes a fictional architecture — any enforcement tests written against the documented topology would pass on non-existent boundaries while missing real ones.

All five fixture sub-zones have zero cross-zone imports, leaving cross-package import graph traversal — the most complex analysis path — with no fixture-level integration test coverage. The fixture corpus tests single-directory file grouping but not the multi-package resolution that distinguishes sourcevision's analysis from a simple directory scanner.

The scripts zone contains two files with cohesion 0: each file is a completely independent node:fs utility with no monorepo package imports. Cohesion 0 in a 2-file zone indicates directory co-location rather than functional grouping — the zone metric is structurally meaningless at this scale and file count, and should be excluded from governance dashboards that use cohesion thresholds to prioritize refactoring.

Zone attribution artifacts (crash misclassification, rex/sourcevision→web-server phantom edges) consistently arise at subdirectory boundaries and nested directory structures. The pattern suggests the community detection algorithm uses directory prefix matching as a fallback when import graph edges are sparse — a heuristic that produces systematically misleading zone assignments for small zones nested inside larger zones.

The web package applies the gateway pattern asymmetrically: viewer schema consumption flows through external.ts (22 consumers, zero bypasses, fully auditable), while server schema consumption in routes-features.ts bypasses any gateway entirely — the same schema types have different governance depending on which zone consumes them, creating an invisible audit gap for server-side schema drift

Both directional coupling metrics between web-server and web-dashboard-ui-hub (88 inbound, 5 outbound) are unaccountable in production source code — architectural decisions based on zone coupling numbers for this pair are operating on faulty data; the root cause (zone attribution algorithm misassigning test or entry-point files) has not been identified and invalidates the quantitative basis for governing this boundary

The prd-tree feature sub-module (tree-search.ts, types.ts, virtual-scroll.ts, prd-tree.ts) forms a tightly coupled internal cluster with 4+ shared import edges but no barrel index.ts — the absence of an internal barrel makes the sub-module's public surface invisible to zone boundary tools and allows external consumers (use-facet-state.ts, views/prd.ts) to reach into component internals directly

routes-features.ts combines HTTP route handling with schema type re-export at the package level — route handler files serving as type distribution points conflate routing and public API surface definition responsibilities, a pattern that if adopted elsewhere would make the server's type export surface ungovernable

The web residual zone (cohesion 0.32) creates a triangular transitive path: web-viewer → web → web-server. This path allows web-viewer to reach web-server through an intermediary zone that bypasses explicit gateway mediation — unlike the direct web-viewer→web-server edge (which is already audited), this transitive route is invisible to boundary-check tooling and undermines the directional clarity that gateways provide. Eliminating the web residual zone by reclassifying its files would remove this bypass path as a side effect.

The web-unit test zone imports web-server internals (3 edges) but not web-viewer. Because web-server holds documented dependencies on viewer types through shared-types.ts, web-unit tests exercise server code that transitively depends on viewer types without making that dependency explicit in their own import graph — an implicit coupling chain invisible to zone analysis that could cause web-unit tests to break from viewer-type changes with no diagnostic import trail.

The three-tier test enforcement pyramid (web-package-integration → monorepo-integration → monorepo-e2e) has no tier that asserts intra-web zone direction constraints outside of web-shared. The crash↔web-viewer topology is the concrete gap: it is documented in CLAUDE.md, identified by zone analysis, fixable in one line, but enforced by zero automated tests across all three tiers.

shared/index.ts (the documented barrel for web-shared) is bypassed by two files that import leaf modules directly: external.ts (../shared/view-id.js, ../shared/data-files.js, ../shared/db-packages.js) and crash-detector.ts (../../shared/view-id.js) — both files import the same symbols re-exported by shared/index.ts, creating a duplicate export surface where changes to shared leaf files must be tracked in three places (leaf file, shared/index.ts, external.ts) instead of two

The crash zone's community detection classification as a separate zone from web-viewer despite being physically located in packages/web/src/viewer/crash/ means zone-level coupling metrics and governance decisions (two-consumer rule, cohesion monitoring) are being applied to what is structurally an intra-zone sub-directory; governance actions targeting the crash↔web-viewer 'cycle' may be solving an artifact rather than a real architectural problem

boundary-check.test.ts enforces crash→web-shared as a documented exemption but has no assertion blocking crash→web-viewer imports. The existing 2-edge crash→web-viewer coupling can grow silently without CI regression. Adding a boundary assertion prohibiting crash from importing web-viewer would close this enforcement gap and prevent the cycle from expanding beyond its current 2-edge scope.

The web residual zone (cohesion 0.32) creates an implicit transitive dependency web-server → web → web-viewer that bypasses the server/viewer boundary enforcement. Because boundary-check only tests direct import edges, this three-hop path is undetectable by current tooling. Reclassifying web zone files into their correct target zones (web-viewer or web-server) would eliminate the transitive path without requiring new gateway code.

The rex-cli zone (27+ command files, coupling 0.75 to rex-prd-engine) has no internal gateway module analogous to web-viewer's external.ts. Each command file independently imports from rex-prd-engine, distributing the cross-zone import surface across 27+ files with no single auditable entry point. A prd-gateway.ts concentrating all rex-prd-engine imports would make coupling regressions visible without reviewing every command file — particularly important given the zone's already-documented dual-fragility designation.

CLAUDE.md's hench sub-zone governance section documents a brief/ directory that does not exist in the source tree, omits six actual sub-zones (guard/, queue/, store/, types/, validation/, schema/), and specifies barrel index.ts requirements that are not met by prd/. The governance documentation is out of sync with the implementation in three distinct ways simultaneously. Any architect or contributor using CLAUDE.md as the authoritative reference for hench's internal structure will make incorrect assumptions about where to place new code.

Zone boundary definitions that use directory prefix matching as a fallback produce phantom edges for zones nested inside larger zones (crash inside src/viewer/, sourcevision micro-zones inside packages/sourcevision/). These phantom edges appear in architectural governance metrics as real coupling violations and cycles, consuming audit effort for non-existent problems. The zone attribution algorithm should distinguish between import-graph-derived boundaries and directory-heuristic boundaries and annotate metrics accordingly.

The fixture corpus for all five fixture sub-zones tests only single-directory file grouping. No fixture exercises cross-package import graph traversal — the code path that resolves imports across module boundaries is the highest-complexity part of sourcevision's analysis pipeline and has zero fixture-level integration coverage. A fixture project where service/ imports from pkg/response/ (or where the TS frontend imports a shared utility) would provide the first non-trivial import graph for fixture-level validation.

The web package has no server-side schema gateway despite schema/ types being consumed by both web-viewer (via external.ts, gateway-enforced) and web-server (routes-features.ts, ungated direct import) — the asymmetry means viewer schema consumption is auditable and enforced while server schema consumption is invisible to boundary-check tooling and domain-isolation tests

Both directional coupling metrics between the two largest web zones (88 web-viewer→web-server imports, 5 web-server→web-viewer imports) are confirmed absent in production source — zone attribution is misclassifying files for this zone pair specifically, making the coupling numbers for the most critical boundary in the web package unreliable for architectural governance

The web residual zone (cohesion 0.32) creates a triangular transitive coupling path: web-viewer → web (7 imports) → web-server (1 import), allowing web-viewer to reach web-server through an uncontrolled intermediary that bypasses gateway mediation. The direct web-viewer→web-server edge is audited and questioned; this transitive route is not — boundary-check.test.ts has no assertion covering web-zone import direction. Reclassifying the residual zone would eliminate the bypass as a structural side effect.

The crash↔web-viewer circular dependency — flagged as the highest-priority structural fix across all analysis passes — has no automated enforcement anywhere in the three-tier test pyramid. After the documented one-line fix in use-crash-recovery.ts, the cycle can re-emerge through any future type-only import from web-viewer into crash without producing a CI failure, because no boundary assertion covers crash zone import direction.

If the detachment is a community detection artifact rather than an intentional boundary, these zones should be reabsorbed by increasing import connectivity to the core.

4964 internal calls across 27 directories could mask a hub file with 200+ internal callers — a hidden fragility point invisible to zone-level analysis. If one analyzer orchestrates all others, changes to it have a blast radius proportional to its caller count, not its import edge count. Generating a per-file internal call count would identify this before the zone needs structural reorganization.
